item.title: It's another unhinged XL Friday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
i put a lot of hours into VC but way more into San Andreas
30 is young for a tortoise
because you want a squid fact
yeah that's pretty good
mmm hmm
truly a lobster lover's dream
aka every day
he's right you know
nice, a cooking fork
those talons
i need to watch this
"do you like Phil Collins?"
lemme get a honk on that bobo
that is a work of art
mmm, forbidden chex..
whoa whoa slow down i need to take notes
that's my time.. have a great weekend, gang!!
item.dc:creator: CeleryMan7

item.title: Where do you think we are?
item.dc:creator: slotheroll

item.title: Exactly one year ago, the world was shocked by the terrible execution of Oleksandr Matsievskyi. The Ukrainian soldier of the 119th TDF, unarmed, who was captured during the battle of Bakhmut, looked the Russian soldiers in the eyes, took a last drag on hi
item.description: and uttered the legendary words "Slava Ukraini". They then brutally shot Matsievskyi to death with automatic rifles from several sides. Matsievskyi, Hero of Ukraine, symbolizes bravery, the continuous struggle to maintain Ukraine's sovereignty and never allow itself to be subjected to Russian imperialism. Let us not forget Matsievskyi, but also all those others who fell for defending a sovereign Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: vladrichdemaclant

item.title: Sci-Fi action heroines
item.description:
Sci-fi is a genre where women long risen to the call. Here are 15 live action bad-asses in sci-fi movies, outside the super hero-genre, in no given order; kicking off with Ellen Ripley (Sigourney Weaver), Alien(s)
Sarah Connor (Linda Hamilton), The Terminator-films
Zoe Washburne (Gina Torres), Serenity
Dizzy Flores (Dina Meyer), Starship Troopers
The Warrior Woman (Virginia Hey), Mad Max 2: The Road Warrior
Kimberly Hart a.k.a. the Pink Ranger (Amy Jo Johnson), Mighty Morphin Power Rangers: The Movie
Dana Scully (Gillian Anderson), The X-Files-films
Rain Ocampo (Michelle Rodriguez), Resident Evil
Trinity (Carrie-Anne Moss), The Matrix-films
Leeloo (Milla Jovovich), The Fifth Element
Rita Vrataski (Emily Blunt), Edge of Tomorrow a.k.a. Live Die Repeat
Cassandra Anderson (Olivia Thirlby), Dredd
Anne Lewis (Nancy Allen), Robocop
Jenette Vasquez (Jenette Goldstein), Aliens
Leia Skywalker Organa (Carrie Fisher), Star Wars-films Edit: this post is not comprahensive, and it has never implied to cover all sci-fi action heroines. If someone you hoped to see is not in the post it may be because it was arbitrarily limited to 15 even though there obviously are many more characters who can be included in a longer list; or it may be because I have myself not seen that character's movie(s); or it may be because the sci-fi heroine was not in a live action sci-fi movie and so falls outside the scope of this post.
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.description: This is Buddy the German Shepherd who led 1st responders to the fire that threatened his families lives. https://www.reddit.com/r/AnimalsBeingBros/comments/ebpvhl/this_is_buddy_the_german_shepherd_who_led_1st/
item.dc:creator: fulcrims

item.title: No man can tell me what to do
item.dc:creator: anthora

item.title: Part 1 of Understanding the fatal virus that changed the world forever.
item.description:
The person in this photo is David Kirby, an AIDS activist who passed away from the disease in 1990. He allowed photojournalist Therese Frare to take these photos of him actively dying from AIDS and his family saying goodbye to him. He died minutes after this photo was taken. I saw a couple of tv/movie scenes that featured someone dying of AIDS, and it deeply affected me. I really wanted to make this post. Especially after finding the pictures of David Kirby. I decided to make it a two-parter: One that’s all about the virus, and another one that chronicles the crisis that this virus caused in the 1980’s and 1990’s. That way those that aren’t interested in reading a lengthier post about history still have the facts about HIV/AIDS, and how to keep safe.
This is why knowing about HIV/AIDS is important. It’s not the dire crisis that it was during the 80’s and 90’s, but it is still a killer. Even in America, people are dying from AIDS - usually due to a lack of affordable healthcare. HIV/AIDS is not a thing of the past. In 2019, HIV/AIDS was the 9th leading cause of death in low income communities worldwide. Above it is tuberculosis, which should be noted because a good portion of HIV/AIDS deaths are a result of tuberculosis.
I have not been able to find any other statistical information from the W.H.O. Website for recent years. If anyone else reading this has a more recent update on worldwide mortality, please share it!
This feels like a painfully dramatic meme, but I needed some sort of transition from the intro to the actual post...
We lost a lot of good people to this horrible disease.
This is the HIV virus under an electron microscope. The yellow dots are virus that is eating away at the T-Cell . Image Source: https://www.verywellhealth.com/hiv-microscopy-in-pictures-48651
Image Source: https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/abs/mapping-aids/seeing-hiv-as-aids/DAC2079EE602167610B2F8FF3DBD4EA2# ↑ Note: Cambridge University posted this picture of the virus under an electron microscope, but the chapter of the book that it came from has a paywall. . This is the original version. I felt like it gave a better visual on the damages it does to a T-Cell than the previous picture, but the caption is difficult to read, and it's black and white. . Caption in Upper Left Corner: The image on the right is a normal T-Cell, which defends the immune system from infection. The image on the left is an HIV infected T-Cell
I tried to color it on GIMP (cheap photoshop program) to make the stark differences between both T-Cells more noticeable. I'm sure I missed some spots, but hopefully this gives a good idea of the way that the virus kills your T-Cells. Onto AIDS...
Basically: You can have HIV and not get AIDS, but you CAN'T get AIDS without having HIV first. That might seem like a confusing jumble of words, so here's an analogy: You can swim in the ocean and not be attacked by a great white shark - but you CAN'T be attacked by a great white shark unless you're swimming in the ocean. You have to have one before you get the other.
I'll explain opportunistic infections in just a moment.
A SUPER-DUPER VERY IMPORTANT NOTE: You can get these infections without having AIDS if you don't take care of your health, or don't get vaccinated. . Meningitis especially will fuck you up if you don't get vaccinated for it. With untreated AIDS, a meningitis vaccine is useless, as the immune system that is reliant on the vaccine is already destroyed. The infection is taking the opportunity of entering the body without resistance.
I tried to include real life pictures of the complications that arise from AIDS, but the HPV-cancers and kidney + liver disease pictures were a little more gruesome. Google at your own risk.
I don't know, and I'm not going to pretend to know. If an expert wants to shed light in the comments, PLEASE DO!! Now let's talk about how the virus does and doesn't spread...
HOW IS HIV SPREAD? HIV/AIDS is spread through bodily fluids such as blood, semen, vaginal fluids, and breast milk. The most common form of transmission is through unprotected sexual activity, which is why it’s considered an STD. . However, that’s not the only way it can be spread. There are a few more: - Sharing needles (typically associated with illegal drug use, but low-income hospitals have also been forced to share needles due to lack of fresh supplies) - Blood transfusion (not an issue in most countries anymore, as all blood is tested before using, but it can happen) - While less common, beware of those non-professional tattooing and piercing, you have no idea where those needles have been, and the virus has spread as a result of their use - Pregnancy, childbirth, and breast feeding from an HIV+ patient - On very rare occasions, HIV can be spread from tongue-kissing IF the HIV+ person has bleeding gums (anyone with bleeding gums probably shouldn’t be tongue kissing anyone anyway, that’s a smidge unsanitary) . . HIV/AIDS CANNOT be transmitted through what is called “casual contact.” - Holding/shaking hands, hugging, dancing, closed-mouth kissing, sharing toilet seats, sharing food and water, or being in close proximity with an HIV/AIDS positive person. - HIV/AIDS also is not spread by insect bites. - You don’t get HIV from donating blood - a clean needle is used every time! . If you’re wondering why mosquitoes can’t spread HIV despite them being blood-sucking little demons: The virus doesn’t infect the insect, so it doesn’t spread when feasting upon another unfortunate soul in its path (can you tell how much I hate mosquitoes?)
Sometimes making memes can blur the pictures I use, so here's the CDC infographic on how HIV spreads and treatment on it.
If you've experienced a high-risk exposure to HIV, go to the emergency room IMMEDIATELY. What's a high-risk exposure? If you have any reason to believe that HIV-infected bodily fluids have entered your body: - Unprotected vaginal or anal sex with known (or likely) HIV positive partner - Injection drug use needle exposure - After a sexual assault (please go to the ER anyway to be checked for other injuries, and get resources from a social worker on how to heal from such a horrific ordeal) - Non-intact skin (open cut or wound) or mucus membrane (eyes, nose, mouth, etc.) contact with blood . . What is nPEP? The Massachussetts state website summarized nPEP as this: Non-occupational post-exposure prophylaxis (nPEP) for HIV is the use of antiretroviral drugs as soon as possible after a high-risk exposure to HIV. Antiretroviral drugs combat HIV infections by attacking the virus' DNA. It helps to reduce (but not eliminate) the possibility of HIV infection. . There's still a chance you could get the virus. You need to know that. But a reduced risk is better than a 100% risk, and that's the important part.
Everything will be okay. You're not going to die a horrible death. There are options. How is HIV/AIDS treated? I'm so glad you asked!
HIV antivirals work by stopping the virus from replicating in the body. With the virus stopped, T-Cells can replenish, and your immune system "restores itself" for lack of a better term. Also, with the virus unable to spread throughout the infected person's body, it can't be spread to others. When the body reaches a point where there is an "undetectable viral load," then it can't be spread to anyone. . THAT BEING SAID: THIS IS NOT A CURE!! You need to take the medications every single day, but if you do, you will get better.
There's often a stigma and shame when it comes to HIV diagnosis and treatment. With antiviral treatment, people with HIV/AIDS have been able to live long and happy lives, find love and friendship, and accomplish their dreams. It is NOT the end. You're also not the only ones that have to take a medication every day to keep healthy. I have bipolar disorder, and take three medications every single day to keep from going crazy. My mother has hypothyroidism, and takes medication every day to keep her thyroid in check. People with diabetes need to inject themselves with insulin to stay alive. You're no different than anyone else with a chronic condition, even IF that chronic condition is HIV/AIDS. You are worthy of love, acceptance, and happiness.
Part two will go into the history of HIV/AIDS and how it changed attitudes towards sex, drugs, and civil rights.
I was able to dig up a couple of resources for what happens after an HIV/AIDS diagnosis: The official HIV government website has a page dedicated to any suicidal thoughts or emotional crisis that might come with a positive diagnosis: https://www.hiv.gov/blog/lifeline-and-988 The CDC website has a page on HIV consultation and referral services: https://www.cdc.gov/hiv/library/hotlines.html Ryan White HIV/AIDS program's list of HIV hotlines for every state in the US: https://ryanwhite.hrsa.gov/hiv-care/hotlines The State of New York has an entire program dedicated to HIV/AIDS testing, resources, and mental health counseling: https://www.gmhc.org/at-a-glance/ The National AIDS Hotline: (800) 342-AIDS / (800) 344-7432 (Spanish) / (800) 243-7889 (TDD)
KFF Overview of HIV/AIDS: https://www.kff.org/global-health-policy/fact-sheet/the-global-hiv-aids-epidemic/ Stages of HIV: https://hivinfo.nih.gov/understanding-hiv/fact-sheets/stages-hiv-infection CDC’s overview of HIV/AIDS: https://www.cdc.gov/hiv/basics/whatishiv.html Mayo Clinic on HIV/AIDS: https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/hiv-aids/symptoms-causes/syc-20373524 World Health Organization’s page on HIV/AIDS: https://www.who.int/news-room/questions-and-answers/item/hiv-aids CDC’s Video on HIV/AIDS: https://youtu.be/vU8-iAB8iQc?si=IJVjedyoVSlKCbUd A cool animation on how HIV/AIDS works in the body: https://youtu.be/ng22Ucr33aw?si=5J46nPvKWTzynh-n How nPEP works, and when/where to get it: https://www.mass.gov/info-details/post-exposure-prophylaxis-npep#:~:text=If%20you%20believe%20you%20have,able%20to%20administer%20nPEP%20onsite. List of Celebrities that we lost to AIDS. I didn’t include all of them, as I could only fit so many on a single page: https://www.hivplusmag.com/people/2021/11/11/celebrities-famous-died-death-aids-hiv#rebelltitem23 This is an interesting video on how antiviral drugs were made, but it’s hard to follow unless you have an education in biology. I couldn’t even follow it well: https://youtu.be/vU8-iAB8iQc?si=IJVjedyoVSlKCbUd The NHS’s website on HIV/AIDS, including how antivirals work: https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/hiv-and-aids/treatment/#:~:text=Antiretroviral%20drugs,quickly%20adapt%20and%20become%20resistant. Here’s the full article on David Kirby: https://time.com/3503000/behind-the-picture-the-photo-that-changed-the-face-of-aids/
item.dc:creator: OlivetheNerd

item.title: O M G
item.description: Dude the word you are looking for is Anesthesia !
item.dc:creator: JustaRandomCanadianFemale

item.title: I have to say goodbye to my beautiful friend.
item.description:
This is maybe my favorite picture of him. He had just bit my other cat Camilla on the ass and shoved her off the bed after she annoyed him into playing with her, and then he turned around looking like a kung fu master with her fur hanging down like a beard. He has kidney failure, if he makes it through the night, i’m taking him into the vet in the morning to say goodbye. Edit - he’s no longer in pain, he rests in the next world now.
He’s lost so much weigh so fast.
Banana and dollar bill for scale. He used to be 20 lbs.
He towered over the other cats in the house.
So big
Enjoyed being a pillow. That’s my friend there.
Pretty in the sunshine.
He loved to lay on my shirts.
Laundry void
Hey, i’m sleeping in here!
He had a great smile
Mlem.
He *HATED* when i would go on a trip. Pouted from the moment i pulled out my suitcase.
Big yawn!
Blowing the scent of some… i think tuna and wet kibble at him after some dental surgery, he woke right up at the smell.
He was tolerant of kittens.
Such a handsome man.
ROUMD
This chatty boy was always waiting for me to come home from work. Sorry for the rotating phone and momentary face
Towering over a soda can
Rare good size comparison with Ashi. They loved my new weighted blanket.
His favorite place was next to me. And he will always be there in my heart.
Floofy toebeans. I think he was part Maine Coon. Not purebred. But fluffy & huge.
When i would step out back on the patio to have a pipe, i was always supervised.
He loved his sunbeam.
Sprawled.
He’s snoring.
This hammock chair has been claimed.
He would join me for meals, he knew i’d give him bits of cheese or chicken.
Loved that sunbeam.
Towering over a soda can again. I honestly loved how LARGE he was. Just a massive ball of affection.
Got his purr here. Happy boy. I’m so glad i got this one recorded.
Sleeping up against me.
Slow blinks and big yawn. He LOVED being brushed.
Watching the world outside. It was rare to catch them together like this.
BIG YAWN. Floofy belly. Gods i’m going to miss him.
Camilla’s idea of “play” was “PLZ ATTACK ME!” She was always purring the entire time.
Moar play
It’s my kit in a box. He loved playing with her.
Always ready for a treat. But polite. He never stole.
Apollo, love, my heart is broken, i would give anything to have more years with you but kidney failure is taking you from me in a few hours and no force on earth can change that. I’m going to miss you so much. You adopted me when i moved out here in 2013, just after my friend’s rescued you from a shelter where, like now, you had hours to live. I will forever treasure the 11 years we had together. That’s one hell of a Bonus Round. Thank you. Thank you for being my friend. I love you. You will always be my first cat. I will love you for the rest of my life. Thank you all for coming on this journey of remembrance with me.
item.dc:creator: tentaclefriendly

item.title: Shrinkflation
item.description: Found at Walmart tonight. This was the only one on the shelf, where they had both the new and old product next to each other. You'd think they'd at least get rid of the old ones first. Same price, less volume on these new bottles. Thanks!

item.dc:creator: PukeC

item.title: Sexist jokes, am I right?
item.description: Today, reflecting on women's day, I thought of a conversation I had with a young man who had just come back from deployment. We sat and talked for a while talking about the females in the army hacing to work twice as hard to earn respect and trust, and after a moment or two, he cracked what to him was a joke about women in the army. Something to do with a woman on their period and how that's scary and wouldn't trust his life with that. His wife turned to him telling him it's not okay, which to his reply was "oh cmon it's just a joke. She gets it." My reply and will always be my reply is "I don't understand, please explain to me why this is funny?" I am telling you, this made the room really quiet. Use it next time you hear a sexist joke. I watched him get uncomfortable and stumble, trying to explain why it's funny. It's never funny. My gender is not a joke. I'm glad to be seen today for women's day.
item.dc:creator: Humangiver

item.title: This brought me great joy on International Women’s Day.
item.dc:creator: TheJusticeMitchell
